title: Bill shock has CEOs eyeing cheaper AI models
full_text:
As Westpac chief executive Anthony Miller works away on his laptop, there is a new addition to his home screen, which reminds him not to get carried away with using artificial intelligence.
An AI token tracker gives him a running tally of how many tokens he is using with his questions to chatbots, strategy simulations and other AI agent tasks. He has told colleagues it is about keeping the fact that each interaction costs money at the front of his mind, so he can judge if Westpac is getting a return on its investment. Like most of his peers, Miller is publicly all-in on AI. When Microsoft boss Satya Nadella visited Australia in April on a global company "AI tour", Miller was a guest speaker at the conference, talking about the bank's plans for its use of AI.
However, Miller is also alive to a growing risk of bill shock from its use, which is increasingly a concern for companies and individuals.
"We want all of our people to be engaged and empowered by AI, not constrained," says Dan Jermyn, Westpac's chief AI officer. "At the same time, it's increasingly important that we use the right tool for the job, given the growing cost of token use."
The cost of AI tokens is why there has been a boom in demand for cheaper Chinese AI models, which in turn is driving a price war between US giants OpenAI and Anthropic. This threatens to undermine their business models and valuations ahead of planning public market listings. Companies p13
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Paul Smith | Technology
heading: Data Science and AI
